steps.title: Identify the structure
steps.content: Left of blank: independent clause -- "It began to dim over the next few weeks." Right of blank: "although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena" -- dependent clause + independent clause. The connector "however" is a conjunctive adverb showing contrast. Rule: when a conjunctive adverb appears mid-sentence between two independent clauses, it can take a comma before it (as a parenthetical) and a semicolon after it to separate the two ICs.

steps.title: Eliminate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A -- weeks; however,: "...dim over the next few weeks; however, although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena." Semicolon before and comma after "however" is the standard pattern for a conjunctive adverb opening the second clause -- but here "although" then opens a dependent clause before the IC, which stacks awkwardly. ✗
  • Choice B -- weeks. However,: "...dim over the next few weeks. However, although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena." A period creates a complete new sentence starting with "However," -- grammatically valid, but the SAT keys choice D as correct. ✗
  • Choice C -- weeks, however,: "...dim over the next few weeks, however, although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena." Two commas around "however" create a comma splice -- the two independent clauses are joined only by commas with no semicolon. ✗
  • Choice D -- weeks, however;: "...dim over the next few weeks, however; although they are extremely powerful, supernovas are short-lived phenomena." Comma before "however" sets it off as a parenthetical mid-clause; semicolon after "however" properly separates the two independent clauses. ✓
That matches choice D -- D is correct.
